title: The Voo Sound: Vibrating the Vagus Nerve Back to Safety
dek: When anger gets trapped in the diaphragm and the jaw locks tight, talking won't release it. The Voo sound is a visceral, low-frequency vibration that reaches down into the pelvic bowl, signaling to your nervous system that the danger has passed.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Body’s Cable: Feeling the Vagus Nerve in the Midnight Stillness

You lie awake at 3 AM, the room thick with the silence that makes every trapped breath louder. Your diaphragm clenches like a fist inside your ribs, a muscle caught between the urge to expand and the grip of something stubborn—anger, yes, but not the fiery kind you might expect. This is the kind of fury that sits heavy and cold, folded into the tissue of your body so deeply that it presses your lungs into shallow, desperate inhales. It’s the tight coil beneath the breastbone, the quiet scream in the spaces between your ribs, where the diaphragm’s broad dome resists the air it was made to welcome. You smell your own skin, a bit salty, a little damp with the residue of night sweat, the scent of tension held without permission.

This isn’t just a feeling floating in the ether or a mood to be managed by willpower alone. It’s a biological fact. The vagus nerve, that long, wandering cable of nerves, threads its way from your brainstem down through your neck, twisting past your heart and lungs, reaching deep into your gut. It’s not a metaphor; it’s a physical highway made of nerve fibers, a thick rope of communication between your head and your belly. When you feel that diaphragm tighten, when your breath hitches and the familiar knot of anxiety settles into your chest, you are feeling the vagus nerve’s distress signals in real time.

The vagus nerve is the body’s grand mediator, its peacekeeper and its alarm bell. But it does not function in some distant, clinical realm. It lives in the fascia that wraps your organs, in the muscles that flex when you breathe, in the connective tissue sheaths that slide and glide beneath your skin. It remembers the moments when you were told your fury was ugly, when you learned to swallow your voice and clamp your jaw. The masseter muscle, tight and clenched at the angle of your jaw, carries the memory of words left unsaid. The trapezius and levator scapulae hitch up around your shoulders, creating a fortress of tension that blocks your ability to fully open your chest. This is not pathology. This is biology.

At 3 AM, when the world feels both still and too loud, your body is insisting on being heard. The shallow breath is a signal, the diaphragm’s refusal to relax a message. You can trace the line of the vagus nerve in your mind’s eye, following its path from the base of your skull, beside the sternocleidomastoid muscle, down past your collarbone, threading through the deep grooves of your thoracic cavity. It’s a physical cable that carries not just electrical impulses but the history of your emotional life, the sediment of every moment you were told to quiet your rage.

This is why you practice the Voo sound—not because it’s a trendy exercise, but because it vibrates along that cable. The sound is a way to touch the nerve directly, to invite the diaphragm to soften, to coax the breath back into its full, nourishing arc. The “voo” isn’t just a sound; it’s medicine. It’s a hum that loosens the fascia, that massages the muscles around the throat and chest, that signals to the vagus nerve that it’s safe to slow down, to rest, to reset.

When you make the Voo sound, you’re not just making noise in the dark. You are speaking to a nerve that carries the weight of your story, a nerve that remembers the tightness in your body and the times you were told to be small. This practice is an act of reclamation, a way to say to your body, “I see you. I hear you. You are not alone in this night.” The breath is no longer shallow. The diaphragm no longer fights for space. The cable hums softly beneath your skin, a quiet current of life running through the midnight stillness.

## The Voo Sound as a Somatic Signal: Vibrating Safety into Your Viscera

You settle into the quiet hours just before dawn, the air holding that damp chill that makes your skin feel a little more awake, more alive. It’s here, in this half-light, that Peter Levine’s teachings on somatic experiencing meet the simple, potent practice of the “Voo” sound. This isn’t just breath or noise—it’s a visceral signal, a message in vibration sent straight to the deep architecture of your nervous system.

Levine, a pioneer in trauma therapy, reminds us that the body remembers what the mind tries to forget. The jaw remembers, the diaphragm remembers, the pelvic floor remembers. When you make the “Voo” sound—soft, rounded, and steady—you engage muscles and fascia that cradle your inner organs. The masseter relaxes subtly as your lips form that oval shape, but it’s what happens deeper inside that counts. The diaphragm descends and vibrates, like a bell tolling safety through the viscera: the liver, the stomach, the gut. This is not just sound; it’s a physical hum that reverberates through your psoas and pelvic floor, the foundational muscles that hold and release tension in the lower body.

Imagine the pelvic floor first: a hammock of muscle and connective tissue, often clenched tight in response to stress and fury unspoken. When you voice “Voo,” your pelvic floor pulses with the vibration, inviting those muscles to soften. The levator ani and coccygeus muscles, which often constrict under chronic tension, respond to the sound’s oscillation, signaling to your nervous system that it’s safe to let go. You can feel this subtle shift—a loosening of the internal grip, a release that your jaw and shoulders might not yet notice.

The diaphragm, that dome-shaped muscle beneath your lungs, is the real powerhouse here. It’s both a gatekeeper and a messenger. When you exhale into the “Voo,” the diaphragm descends fully, creating a low, rounded tone. This vibration pulses through the viscera, massaging the organs gently from below. It’s a communication the nervous system reads instantly: “No threat here.” The parasympathetic nervous system—the branch that calms and restores—takes the cue. Your trapezius and levator scapulae, often knotted with the residue of anger and fear, begin their slow unwinding as the safety signal sinks deeper.

What you’re doing is less about forcing relaxation and more about coaxing your biology back into balance. The “Voo” sound doesn’t ask your nervous system to pretend or to override its protective instincts—it reminds it of what safety feels like in muscle and fascia. The viscera vibrate, the diaphragm pulses, the pelvic floor softens, and the nervous system registers a moment of reprieve. This is not pathology, this is biology.

Levine’s somatic experiencing teaches that trauma lives in the body’s musculature and connective tissue, held in patterns of bracing and contraction. The “Voo” sound practice offers a way to shift these patterns through sound and sensation, without asking your mind to do all the work. You’re not just practicing breath control—you’re speaking the language of your body’s deep safety.

The scent of wet earth on your skin mingles with the faint herbal notes of peppermint and chamomile, which you might have steeped earlier to support this practice. These plants have their own wisdom about soothing the nervous system, but it’s the vibration of your own voice that carries the most potent medicine. As the “Voo” hums through your viscera, you feel the slow unwinding start in your pelvis, the diaphragm’s dome lifting and falling like a tide, the jaw unclenching its centuries-old grip. In this moment, your body knows: it is safe to be here, safe to feel, safe to rest.

## Voo Sound Practice: Settling the Nervous System Through Voice and Herb

You start with the body in a familiar place — the masseter muscles clenched, the trapezius coiled up near the base of your neck like a tightly wound spring, the diaphragm shallow and hesitant in its movement. This is sympathetic overdrive, the fight-or-flight state where the body is primed for action, but you’re stuck in a moment of tension that doesn’t resolve. Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal theory shows us that your vagus nerve isn’t just a single line but a complex system with distinct branches: the dorsal vagal, which can shut down or freeze you, and the ventral vagal, which holds your capacity for social engagement and safety. The shift you want is from that adrenalized clench to the softening of ventral vagal tone — where your jaw unclenches, your neck softens, and your breath deepens, moving through the diaphragm’s full arc.

The 'Voo' sound practice is a tender, physical tool to help this happen. When you hum out the 'voo,' your vocal cords gently vibrate and stimulate the vagal pathways, especially the ventral vagus running through your voice box and pharynx. You feel the subtle massage of the vagus nerve fibers as the sound rolls out, the fascia around your larynx loosening, the levator scapulae at the back of your neck releasing its habitual grip. The body knows this tune; the jaw remembers how to unclench, not because you force it, but because the sound invites it. The breath lengthens, flowing past your lips with a soft buzzing, the kind of resonance that carries through your sinuses and chest, waking the parasympathetic system like a slow tide. This is not pathology, this is biology — a built-in reset button in the nervous system that your voice can press.

Alongside this vocal work, certain nervine herbs provide a physical ally for the transition. Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) is one of those herbs that speaks directly to the nervous system’s frayed edges. It’s not a sedative that knocks you out but a tonic that calms the jittery nerves — the masseter’s trembling, the psoas muscle’s anxious tightness — encouraging the body to settle into ventral vagal safety. When you take Skullcap as a tincture, you might notice a subtle shift in your body’s temperature, a gentle loosening in your throat and the tendons around your jaw, as if the plant’s medicine whispers to the fascia, coaxing it to unwind.

Motherwort (Leonurus cardiaca) is another stern but tender friend in this process. Traditionally known as a heart herb, it’s no sentimental flower; it works on the cardiac branch of the vagus nerve, supporting the rhythmic ebb and flow of your heartbeat, which is central to the ventral vagal state. Your heart rate variability improves, the levator scapulae lets go of its chronic tension, and the diaphragm finds its full range again. Taking Motherwort tincture during your 'voo' sound practice helps root the shift in a deeper, more embodied way — it’s the difference between a momentary sigh and a sustained, felt safety. You can find a reliable tincture like [FES Pink Yarrow](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B009FM6P6M?tag=spankyspinola-20) that embodies this medicine.

In the late evening, as the sky dims and the air cools, this practice becomes a ritual — your voice humming the 'voo' under your breath, the herbal tincture warming your chest and belly. You notice the masseter no longer clenches in the same way, the trapezius softens, and with that, the body releases the old story of fury as something ugly and instead simply moves into its biology of safety and connection. The Polyvagal theory isn’t just academic here; it’s the lived experience in your jaw, your breath, your heart, and the herbs that stand beside you. The nervous system shifts in real time, guided by sound, touch, and plant allies — a somatic symphony that reclaims your body’s inherent capacity to rest, reset, and feel safe once again.

## Voo Sound Practice: Settling Into Resonance

You start by finding yourself in a quiet corner of your day, preferably just as the late afternoon leans into the early evening, when the light softens and your body’s edges begin to soften along with it. Sit on a firm cushion or a chair that supports your sit bones without forcing the chest forward. Your feet rest flat on the floor, grounding you like the roots of an old tree. Your spine grows tall—not stiff, but upright enough that your breath can move freely through the diaphragm, that dome of muscle under your ribs that often gets hijacked by tension. The diaphragm is your ally here, and you want to invite it to expand gently with each inhale, and contract with each exhale, without effort or force.

Now, soften your jaw. The masseter muscles, those powerful jaw muscles that clench when you’re stressed, need to loosen. Let your lips part slightly, just enough that your teeth don’t touch and your tongue rests lightly against the roof of your mouth, just behind your front teeth. This position creates space for the sound to resonate, for the vibrations to ripple through your oral cavity and beyond.

Begin by inhaling slowly through your nose, filling the belly before the chest. Feel the diaphragm press downward, your abdomen softening like the tide pulling away from the shore. As you exhale, produce the sound “Voo.” It’s a low, humming sound, with the lips rounded but relaxed—like the shape of a small O, soft and pliable, not tight or forced. The “V” is voiced, so your vocal cords engage in a gentle buzz, not a shout or a whisper.

As you sound “Voo,” pay attention to where you feel the vibration. The most immediate place is the front of your face—around your lips and the upper palate—but don’t stop there. Let the vibration sink down through the jaw muscles, especially the masseter and temporalis, releasing any clenching. You might notice a buzz in the hyoid bone, that floating bone in your throat that supports the tongue and the floor of the mouth. If you’re really tuned in, you can feel the vibration traveling down through the pharynx and into the upper chest, where the diaphragm gently presses outwards.

Your belly should remain soft throughout this process, never braced or held rigid. If you notice the psoas tensing—a deep core muscle that connects the lumbar spine to the femur—just remind yourself to breathe into the belly again, letting it rise and fall like a slow wave. The trapezius and levator scapulae muscles around your neck and shoulders should feel like they’re melting away, the tension dissolving with each hum.

Try to keep the sound sustained for as long as feels natural—don’t strain. The goal is a steady, even “Voo” that moves with your breath, not against it. When you reach the end of your exhale, release the sound and allow your body to settle into the silence, noticing the subtle afterglow of vibration still humming through your tissues.

Repeat this cycle five to seven times, each time tuning deeper into the sensations. You might catch the scent of your own breath, a faint earthiness mixed with the subtle musk of the skin at the back of your throat. This is your body remembering how to relax, how to reset through sound and breath.

If your mind wanders—which it will—gently bring your attention back to the vibration in the jaw and chest, to the soft rounding of your lips, to the belly’s gentle rise and fall. This is not about performance or perfection; it’s about the body’s innate wisdom, the jaw’s memory of release, the diaphragm’s invitation to breathe fully again.

Over time, this practice builds a subtle shift in your nervous system. The vagus nerve, that long wandering messenger of calm, responds to the hum, to the vibration, to the deliberate softness of your sound. You’re not just making noise—you’re sending a signal of safety and ease, rewiring old pathways of tension and guarding. This is the biology of relaxation, the body’s own medicine, humming quietly in the fading daylight.

## FAQ

**Q:** I feel silly making the "Voo" sound out loud. Is that normal?  
**A:** Absolutely. The masseter and the small muscles around your lips are not used to this precise, slow vibration. The levator labii superioris, the tiny muscle that lifts your upper lip, might feel awkward, even rebellious. You’re reawakening muscles that have been dormant or constrained by years of holding fury back. The body remembers the jaw’s old habits, the tight clenching that said, “don’t speak up.” Feeling silly is just your nervous system’s way of telling you it’s encountering something new—this is not a sign to stop, but an invitation to lean into the discomfort and let the sound ripple through the diaphragm and up the vagus nerve. The evening, when the air cools and the chest muscles soften, is a perfect time to practice, with a blanket or a cup of warm chrysanthemum tea nearby to ground you.

**Q:** I don’t feel any vibration in my throat or chest when I do the practice. Am I doing it wrong?  
**A:** The vibrations you’re seeking live in the fascia and subtle muscle fibers—the thyroarytenoid of the vocal cords, the cricothyroid muscle, even the diaphragm’s gentle contraction beneath your ribs. If you’re not feeling it, your nervous system might still be guarding, holding the trapezius and levator scapulae rigid in anticipation. Try slowing the sound, making it softer, less forced. Close your eyes and place a hand lightly on your throat or chest to tune in to the faintest hum. The body knows how to vibrate; it’s the jaw and throat remembering freedom, not pathology. Over time, as your diaphragm softens in the quiet hours before dawn, those vibrations will become unmistakable, like a secret language between your nervous system and your breath.

**Q:** Sometimes I start crying suddenly during or after the practice. Why does that happen?  
**A:** Tears are the body’s release valve for held tension, especially in the psoas and diaphragm, where fury, grief, and ancient survival patterns lodge. This is not weakness or a sign you’re broken; it’s biology. The vagal reset stirs deep reservoirs of emotion, loosening the fascia and softening the jaw that’s been clenched in silence. When you let the “Voo” sound ripple through your body, you’re bypassing the cerebral defenses and speaking directly to the autonomic nervous system. Tears mean the system is recalibrating, the jaw remembering it can unclench, the diaphragm learning to breathe freely again. Let the tears come; they’re medicine as potent as any herb. Keep a chamomile infusion at hand and allow your body the time it needs to settle afterward.

**Q:** Can I do this practice anytime, or is there a best time?  
**A:** The body’s rhythms matter. Early morning, when the night’s stillness softens the diaphragm and the masseter releases its grip, is ideal. Evening, when the day’s tension settles into the trapezius and levator scapulae, is also good. Avoid times when you’re overstimulated—your nervous system needs to come into a receptive state, not a reactive one. Pay attention to how your body responds, and trust that the jaw remembers when it’s ready to speak again.
